title: Cottage loaf
subtitle: The twin-tier British white loaf
excerpt: A classic British white loaf shaped as two rounds stacked and dimpled together, with a crackling crust and a soft, chewy crumb. One of the oldest recognised shapes in British baking.
sourceNotes: Mrs Beeton, Book of Household Management (1861), Project Gutenberg #10136, the cottage loaf appears as the 'common household bread' shape, baked in two rounds. Florence White, Good Things in England (1932): describes the shape and the dimpling technique for joining the two rounds so the top doesn't slip.

content.text: The cottage loaf is two rounds of white dough stacked and dimpled together before baking. The shape is as functional as it looks: more crust per gram of crumb than a tin loaf, and a flat base that sits cleanly on a wooden board. The top round is typically a third of the total dough weight; the bottom round takes the rest.

Domestic ovens drift by 20°C or more. An oven thermometer takes the guessing out. For a cottage loaf the initial high temperature is what sets the shape and builds the crust, a cool oven produces a dense, pale result.

Combine {{strong-bread-flour}}, {{yeast-fast-action}}, and {{salt-table}} in a large bowl (salt and yeast can sit next to each other before you add the water, only prolonged contact before liquid is added causes a problem). Pour in the {{water}} and mix until a shaggy dough forms. Turn out onto an unfloured surface and knead for 10 minutes until smooth and elastic. The dough should spring back when you poke it and pass the windowpane test: stretch a small piece between your fingers; it should thin to a translucent sheet without tearing.
Place the dough in a lightly oiled bowl, cover with a damp cloth or cling film, and leave in a warm spot for
until doubled in size, around 1 hour. The time will vary with room temperature: 20°C takes longer than 25°C. Judge by size, not the clock.

Turn the dough out and weigh it. Set aside one-third (about 165 g) for the top. Shape the larger piece into a tight ball by pulling the sides underneath and pinching the seam closed. Place seam-side down on a lined baking tray. Shape the smaller piece the same way and set it on top, seam-side down. Push two fingers straight down through the centre of both pieces until you feel the tray, this pins the top round to the bottom. Without this step the top separates during the oven spring and rolls off.

Cover loosely with oiled cling film and leave to prove for 30 minutes. Heat the oven to 230°C (210°C fan) with a small roasting tray on the bottom shelf. The loaf is ready when it springs back slowly when pressed, if it springs back immediately it needs more time; if the indent stays it has over-proved.

Pour 200 ml of cold water into the hot roasting tray to create steam. Dust the loaf with flour and slash around the shoulder of the top round with a sharp knife or lame. Bake for 15 minutes, then remove the water tray, drop the temperature to 200°C (180°C fan), and bake for a further 20 minutes. The loaf is done when it sounds hollow when tapped on the base and reads 96°C on an instant-read thermometer. Cool on a wire rack for at least 30 minutes before cutting, the crumb is still setting.

items.fix: Push two fingers all the way to the tray before proving. If the oven spring is still splitting the join, reduce the initial temperature by 10°C.
items.cause: The finger-press didn't go deep enough, or the prove was too short and the oven spring was violent.
items.symptom: Top round separated and fell off

items.fix: Ten minutes of firm hand-kneading should do it. Test with the windowpane stretch. Allow the bulk ferment to run until the dough is visibly puffy and doubled, regardless of time.
items.cause: Under-kneading (insufficient gluten development) or bulk ferment cut short before the dough had doubled.

items.fix: Verify the oven temperature with a thermometer. Steam prevents the surface drying out before the oven spring is complete, which allows a thinner, crackling crust.
items.cause: Oven too cool or no steam in the first stage.
